text: The surgeon marks all portions of the neobladder with 3-0 undyed vicryl sutures. Moving distally towards the ileocecal valve, an 11-centimeter segment of bowel is isolated and marked as the zero cm point. This segment serves as a reference for measuring the remaining proximal portions of the small bowel for neobladder construction. Bowel segments not used for diversion are marked with 3-0 dyed vicryl sutures.

The surgeon identifies the thirty-three centimeter point on the bowel, with additional bowel visible below and behind the eleven centimeter point. The forty-four centimeter point is reached, marking the end of the bowel segment needed for pouch formation. The discard segment is measured and divided using a sixty millimeter laparoscopic stapler, then removed.
The surgeon utilizes the discard segment to create a mesenteric window, carefully avoiding deep dissection into the root of the mesentery.

The surgeon locates the portions of ileum marked with dyed two-zero vicryl sutures for anastomosis. The intestinal loops are aligned and an intracorporeal side-to-side anastomosis is performed using a sixty millimeter laparoscopic stapler.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to retract the apex of the posterior plate toward the pelvis. A 24 French chest tube is inserted into the isolated intestine, and the bowel is detubularized. A bias incision is made toward the medial mesenteric edge of the intestine using electrocautery.

The surgeon aligns the medial edges of the bivalve intestine and places interrupted sutures to maintain symmetry of the posterior plate. Barbed sutures are used to keep tension, ensuring the edges align for a watertight seal. Running sutures are applied from distal to proximal, using a two-zero V-lock stitch on a CT-one needle. The posterior plate of the neobladder is rotated ninety degrees counterclockwise to align the urethro-ileal anastomosis point with the urethra.

The surgeon performs the neobladder urethral anastomosis by suturing the ileal edge to the posterior six o'clock part of the urethral stump, continuing anteriorly in a running fashion over a 22 French Cavalier urethral catheter up to the twelve o'clock position. Interrupted sutures are placed to maintain symmetry and facilitate handling, with each suture being six inches long.

The surgeon folds the lateral edges of the posterior plate anteriorly and closes them with a running suture up to the chimney using a 2-0 V-lock stitch on a CT-1 needle, forming a spherical configuration for the pouch. The assistant holds the sutures to ensure the edges align properly and achieve a watertight seal.

The surgeon performs the ureteral chimney anastomosis by bringing the left ureter to the right side under the sigmoid colon. The distal ends of the ureters are resected and tailored to fit the anastomosis location at the chimney. The ureteral ends are spatulated, and a small enterotomy is created in the afferent limb. A brick-like anastomosis is performed using a four-zero Vicryl and RB-one needle with running sutures over a seven French double J stent. The stent is inserted through a two millimeter mini port in the right inferior quadrant. The proximal end of the chimney is over sown with a three-zero Vicryl running suture to prevent stone formation.

The surgeon fills the neobladder pouch with 180 milliliters of saline to visually confirm a watertight closure, ensuring there is no leakage. Indocyanine green (ICG) may be used to assess the vascularity of the neobladder if there is any doubt.
